original_abstract:
The trials and tribulations inherent in religious service are omnipresent, often leading many practitioners to withdraw or falter in their divine calling. For those specifically designated as servants of God, these challenges can become overwhelming obstacles to faithful ministry. This study employs a qualitative approach to provide an exegetical exposition of 2 Timothy 1:1-18, arguing for the necessity of a robust "theology of perseverance" among the clergy. By focusing on Paul’s exhortations to Timothy, the research establishes perseverance as a foundational pillar for contemporary pastoral theology.

The investigation seeks to deconstruct the socio-psychological context of Timothy’s life and the ecclesiastical environment of Ephesus to better understand the unique theological pressures he faced. Findings indicate that steadfastness is not merely a personal trait but a central ministerial requirement articulated through Paul’s apostolic counsel. Through a rigorous textual analysis, this article identifies the essential characteristics of a resilient servant of God, offering insights into the nature of the divine calling and the specific hurdles of spiritual leadership. Ultimately, this research enriches both the theoretical and practical dimensions of Christian service, encouraging modern church leaders to integrate the enduring principles of 2 Timothy into their current ministerial frameworks.
humanized_abstract:
Every pastor is conscious of the problems involved in what may be called pastoral ministry. Problems which are so great that they serve as a great disincentive to large numbers of biblical pastors. So here we have the challenges to God’s servants which hinder them from the effective performance of their ministry as God’s servants. This dissertation sets out to make a qualitative exegesis of 2 Timothy 1:1-18 through investigating the need for what can be termed a ‘theology of perseverance’ for contemporary pastoral ministry using the challenges Paul made on to Timothy and thus positing perseverance as a major component of pastoral theology.

The study attempts to identify the socio-psychological factors of Timothy’s life and the ecclesiastical situation in Ephesus that influenced his theological crisis. The study shows that endurance is not a personal quality but a mandate of the ministry which Paul explicitly stipulates in his exhortations to Timothy. Through a detailed exegesis of the text of 2 Timothy, the study attempts to identify the characteristics of an effective minister of the Gospel and provides insights into the nature of the divine call and the challenges of the spiritual ministry. The study aims at contributing to both the theoretical and practical aspects of the Christian ministry. Today’s church leaders will be motivated to emulate the enduring principles set out in 2 Timothy.
